key_metrics_output: particle characterization (size, shape, polymer type), effect mechanisms, toxicological endpoints (survival, reproduction, growth, feeding, emergence, embryonic development, mobility, physiology), sub-organismal effects (inflammation, DNA damage, oxidative damage), dose-response relationships, quality assessment scores
abstract: This critical review assessed the quality of 105 microplastic effect studies with aquatic biota by developing 20 quality criteria across four categories: particle characterization, experimental design, applicability in risk assessment, and ecological relevance. Studies scored an average of 44.6% of maximum quality score, with no study meeting all criteria. Key findings identified three robustly supported effect mechanisms: inhibition of food assimilation/decreased nutritional value, internal physical damage, and external physical damage. Major recommendations include improved background contamination verification, more environmentally realistic exposure conditions, and better alignment between tested and environmentally relevant particle sizes. The review emphasizes urgent need for standardized methods and quality assurance in microplastic ecotoxicology research.

particle_size_range: Studies were only included when at least one type of MP tested had a diameter between 1 µm and 5 mm
key_metrics_output: developed 20 quality criteria in four main criteria categories (particle characterization, experimental design, applicability in risk assessment, and ecological relevance)

effects observed following exposure to MP on an organism can either be initiated due to sorption of the particles on the external surface of the organism or due to other mechanisms of action being triggered following their ingestion
effects of MPs can occur at the community level, population level, individual level, or sub-organismal level
abstract: reviewed 105 microplastic effect studies with aquatic biota, provided a systematic overview of their characteristics, developed 20 quality criteria
On average, studies scored 44.6% (range 20-77.5%) of the maximum score. No study scored positively on all criteria
three of these mechanisms remained. These are inhibition of food assimilation and/or decreased nutritional value of food, internal physical damage and external physical damage
